questions.question: What makes a file a module in TypeScript?
options: B) Having at least one import or export statement
C) Being inside a src/ directory
D) Having a module declaration at the top

questions.question: What is the difference between a named export and a default export?
options: A) Named exports must use 'let', default exports use 'const'
B) Named exports are imported by exact name (or alias), default exports can be imported with any name
C) You can only have named exports in a file
D) Default exports are faster at runtime

questions.question: How many default exports can a single module have?
Which syntax correctly imports a named export called 'greet' from './utils'?
Which syntax correctly imports a default export from './logger'?
What is a barrel file in TypeScript?
options: A) A file that contains all the project's tests
B) An index.ts file that re-exports from multiple modules to simplify imports
C) A configuration file for the TypeScript compiler
D) A file that stores environment variables
B) import { add } from './math'; import { greet } from './strings';
D) Both A and B work

questions.question: What does 'export *' do?
options: A) Exports the default export only
B) Re-exports all named exports from the specified module
C) Exports everything including private variables
D) Creates a wildcard type export

questions.question: What is the result of: import * as utils from './helpers'?
options: A) Imports the default export as 'utils'
B) Imports all named exports as properties of the 'utils' namespace object
C) Creates a copy of the module

questions.question: How do you rename a named import?
options: B) import { original as renamed } from './mod';

questions.question: What does 'export type' do in TypeScript?
options: A) Exports the type of a variable
B) Marks the export as type-only, ensuring it is erased during compilation
D) Creates a runtime type object

questions.question: What happens if you try to use a non-exported function from another module?
options: A) It works but shows a warning
B) It causes a compile-time error
C) It returns undefined at runtime
D) It throws a runtime error

questions.question: In a multi-file project, which tsconfig.json option specifies the output directory for compiled JavaScript?
What is the difference between 'import type { X }' and 'import { X }'?
options: B) 'import type' is only for classes
C) 'import type' is erased at compile time and cannot be used as a value
D) 'import type' is faster at runtime

questions.question: Which module system does TypeScript use by default when targeting modern JavaScript?

What does this re-export do?
export { default as myLogger } from './logger';
options: A) Exports the default export of './logger' as a named export called 'myLogger'
B) Creates a new default export called 'myLogger'

questions.question: What happens when you import a module that has side effects (e.g., console.log at top level)?
options: A) Side effects run every time the module is imported
B) Side effects run only the first time the module is loaded
D) Side effects cause a compile error

questions.question: How do you import a module only for its side effects?
In a barrel file (index.ts), which pattern is commonly used to re-export everything from a sub-module?
What is circular dependency?
options: A) When a module imports itself
B) When two or more modules import each other directly or indirectly
C) When a module exports and imports the same name
D) When imports form a tree structure
B) Compile error: cannot assign to a const

questions.question: Which file extension should you use in import paths when using Node.js ES module resolution with TypeScript?
What does 'export =' syntax represent in TypeScript?
What advantage does a barrel file provide?
options: B) Cleaner import paths by consolidating exports into a single entry point
A) Yes, barrel files export everything automatically
B) No, only 'add' was re-exported from the barrel file
C) Yes, but only with 'import * as'
D) No, this causes a circular dependency
